English.first-intro: You are an American tourist visiting Spain, chatting with your friend Miguel about football and family. He is curious about what you watch and play back home. Here is how you say: I play football video games
English.second-intro: Say: I play football video games
English.question: I play football video games

English.first-intro: You also want to tell Miguel about your football habits. Listen and repeat: I also watch football on TV from time to time
English.second-intro: Say: I also watch football on TV from time to time
English.question: I also watch football on TV from time to time

Spanish.answer: Yo también veo el fútbol en la tele de vez en cuando

English.first-intro: Now put it together, how would you say: my son often watches football with me
English.second-intro: Now try to say: my son often watches football with me
English.question: my son often watches football with me

Spanish.answer: Mi hijo a menudo ve el fútbol conmigo

English.first-intro: This is the phrase for: to watch it
English.second-intro: How do you say: to watch it
English.question: to watch it

English.first-intro: Using what you have learned, tell Miguel: we like to watch it together
English.second-intro: Put it together: we like to watch it together
English.question: we like to watch it together

Spanish.grammar: gustar with indirect object, infinitive + object pronoun

English.first-intro: Add what you do with your son, how would you say: and I play football video games with my son
English.second-intro: Add: and I play football video games with my son
English.question: and I play football video games with my son

Spanish.answer: Y juego a videojuegos de fútbol con mi hijo

English.first-intro: Miguel asks about football back home. Here is the question: is it popular in the United States?
English.second-intro: Ask: is it popular in the United States?
English.question: is it popular in the United States?

English.first-intro: Answer him by comparing the two places: not as popular as here
English.second-intro: Answer him: not as popular as here
English.question: not as popular as here

English.first-intro: Miguel shifts to your family and asks about your younger nephew: and what about your younger nephew?
English.second-intro: Ask: and what about your younger nephew?
English.question: and what about your younger nephew?

Spanish.grammar: question word, a + person clarifier, comparative menor/mayor

English.first-intro: He follows up about your nephew, asking: does he like (it)?
English.second-intro: Ask about him: does he like (it)?
English.question: does he like (it)?

Spanish.grammar: question word, a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object

English.first-intro: Now make the question specific, how would you ask: does he like football?
English.second-intro: Ask: does he like football?
English.question: does he like football?

Spanish.grammar: question word, a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel the answer about your younger nephew: he doesn’t play football
English.second-intro: Say: he doesn’t play football
English.question: he doesn’t play football

English.first-intro: Add what he does instead: he watches it on TV
English.second-intro: Say: he watches it on TV
English.question: he watches it on TV

English.first-intro: Now include the contrast and frequency: but he often watches it on TV
English.second-intro: Add the contrast: but he often watches it on TV
English.question: but he often watches it on TV

Spanish.answer: Pero a menudo lo ve en la tele

English.first-intro: Your older nephew is watching a match right now. Here is how you say: right now my older nephew is watching a football match on TV
English.second-intro: Say: right now my older nephew is watching a football match on TV
English.question: right now my older nephew is watching a football match on TV

Spanish.grammar: estar + gerund, possessive adjective, comparative menor/mayor
Spanish.answer: Ahora mismo, mi sobrino mayor está viendo un partido de fútbol en la tele

English.first-intro: Shorten that idea and say: he is watching it
English.second-intro: Say: he is watching it
English.question: he is watching it

English.first-intro: Add what the younger one does too: and the younger one also watches a lot of football
English.second-intro: Add: and the younger one also watches a lot of football
English.question: and the younger one also watches a lot of football

Spanish.grammar: present tense, también / tampoco, comparative menor/mayor
Spanish.answer: Y el menor también ve mucho fútbol

English.first-intro: You and Miguel are now browsing a sports shop for football shirts. The first item to name is: a team
English.second-intro: How do you say: a team
English.question: a team

English.first-intro: Build on that phrase, how would you say: a football team
English.second-intro: Say: a football team
English.question: a football team

English.first-intro: Now put the idea into a shirt: the football team shirt
English.second-intro: Say: the football team shirt
English.question: the football team shirt

Spanish.answer: Una camiseta de un equipo de fútbol

English.first-intro: Ask Miguel where to buy it, how would you say: where can I buy a T-shirt of a football team?
English.second-intro: Ask: where can I buy a T-shirt of a football team?
English.question: where can I buy a T-shirt of a football team?

Spanish.grammar: question word, poder + infinitive, de + noun
Spanish.answer: ¿Dónde puedo comprar una camiseta de un equipo de fútbol?

English.first-intro: Miguel answers with a nearby option: there’s a sporting goods store
English.second-intro: Say: there’s a sporting goods store
English.question: there’s a sporting goods store

Spanish.answer: Hay una tienda de artículos deportivos

English.first-intro: He adds how close it is: only three blocks from here
English.second-intro: Add: only three blocks from here
English.question: only three blocks from here

English.first-intro: Miguel politely tells you what you can do there: there you can buy sporting goods
English.second-intro: Say politely: there you can buy sporting goods
English.question: there you can buy sporting goods

English.first-intro: Explain who the shirt is for: the football team shirt is for my older son
English.second-intro: Say: the football team shirt is for my older son
English.question: the football team shirt is for my older son

Spanish.grammar: para + person, possessive adjective, comparative menor/mayor
Spanish.answer: La camiseta de un equipo de fútbol es para mi hijo mayor

English.first-intro: Say why the shirt makes sense: he likes football a lot
English.second-intro: Say: he likes football a lot
English.question: he likes football a lot

Spanish.grammar: a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object
Spanish.answer: A él le gusta mucho el fútbol

English.first-intro: You start thinking aloud, how would you say: maybe I can buy something
English.second-intro: Try saying: maybe I can buy something
English.question: maybe I can buy something

English.first-intro: Now you think about your younger son, who has different interests. Try saying: maybe I can buy something for my younger son
English.second-intro: Say: maybe I can buy something for my younger son
English.question: maybe I can buy something for my younger son

Spanish.grammar: subjunctive with quizás, poder + infinitive, para + person
Spanish.answer: Quizás pueda comprar algo para mi hijo menor

English.first-intro: Here is a useful way to begin a guess: maybe there is
English.second-intro: How do you say: maybe there is
English.question: maybe there is

English.first-intro: Extend that guess, how would you say: maybe there’s something
English.second-intro: Say: maybe there’s something
English.question: maybe there’s something

English.first-intro: Place the idea in the shop: in the sporting goods store
English.second-intro: Say: in the sporting goods store
English.question: in the sporting goods store

Spanish.answer: En la tienda de artículos deportivos

English.first-intro: Put it all together for your younger son: maybe there’s something for my younger son
English.second-intro: Put it together: maybe there’s something for my younger son
English.question: maybe there’s something for my younger son

Spanish.grammar: subjunctive with quizás, hay, para + person
Spanish.answer: Quizás haya algo para mi hijo menor

English.first-intro: Clarify the reason, how would you say: as for him, he doesn’t like football
English.second-intro: Say: as for him, he doesn’t like football
English.question: as for him, he doesn’t like football

Spanish.grammar: negation, a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object
Spanish.answer: A él no le gusta el fútbol

English.first-intro: Shorten the idea about him: he doesn’t like (it)
English.second-intro: Say: he doesn’t like (it)
English.question: he doesn’t like (it)

Spanish.grammar: negation, a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object

English.first-intro: The hobby you want to mention is fishing. How would you say: to fish
English.second-intro: How do you say: to fish
English.question: to fish

English.first-intro: Add the contrast about your younger son: but he likes to fish
English.second-intro: Add: but he likes to fish
English.question: but he likes to fish

Spanish.grammar: gustar with indirect object, present tense

English.first-intro: Here is the phrase you need for a fishing gift: for fishing
English.second-intro: How do you say: for fishing
English.question: for fishing

English.first-intro: Now say what the store might have: maybe there’s something for fishing
English.second-intro: Try saying: maybe there’s something for fishing
English.question: maybe there’s something for fishing

Spanish.grammar: subjunctive with quizás, hay, para + infinitive

English.first-intro: Your daughter has her own hobby. Listen and repeat: to swim
English.second-intro: How do you say: to swim
English.question: to swim

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel about your daughter: and my daughter likes to swim
English.second-intro: Say: and my daughter likes to swim
English.question: and my daughter likes to swim

Spanish.grammar: a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object
Spanish.answer: Y a mi hija le gusta nadar

English.first-intro: Say that the shop may have a gift for her as well: maybe there’s something for her too
English.second-intro: Say: maybe there’s something for her too
English.question: maybe there’s something for her too

Spanish.grammar: subjunctive with quizás, hay, para + person
Spanish.answer: Quizás haya algo para ella también

English.first-intro: Here is the short phrase: for her
English.second-intro: How do you say: for her
English.question: for her

English.first-intro: Miguel turns the question back to you politely: and for you?
English.second-intro: Ask politely: and for you?
English.question: and for you?

English.first-intro: The shop reminds him of tourist gifts. Here is how you say: a souvenir
English.second-intro: How do you say: a souvenir
English.question: a souvenir

English.first-intro: Miguel asks whether you want one, how would he say: don’t you want a souvenir of Spain?
English.second-intro: Ask: don’t you want a souvenir of Spain?
English.question: don’t you want a souvenir of Spain?

English.first-intro: He suggests another souvenir idea: maybe a photo of a flamenco dancer?
English.second-intro: Suggest: maybe a photo of a flamenco dancer?
English.question: maybe a photo of a flamenco dancer?

English.first-intro: Tell him no, because you are shopping for your wife: no, but I need something for my wife
English.second-intro: Answer him: no, but I need something for my wife
English.question: no, but I need something for my wife

Spanish.answer: No, pero necesito algo para mi mujer

English.first-intro: The errand widens into dinner shopping at a market. The first groceries to name are: artichokes and asparagus
English.second-intro: Say: artichokes and asparagus
English.question: artichokes and asparagus

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel what you need to buy: I need to buy vegetables, artichokes and asparagus
English.second-intro: Say: I need to buy vegetables, artichokes and asparagus
English.question: I need to buy vegetables, artichokes and asparagus

Spanish.answer: Necesito comprar verduras, alcachofas y espárragos

English.first-intro: Explain why you need them: for a dinner at the house of my friend
English.second-intro: Say: for a dinner at the house of my friend
English.question: for a dinner at the house of my friend

Spanish.answer: Para cenar, en casa de mi amigo

English.first-intro: Miguel says he also has an errand: I have to go shopping too
English.second-intro: Say: I have to go shopping too
English.question: I have to go shopping too

Spanish.answer: Yo tengo que ir también de compras

English.first-intro: You guess the purpose and ask him: in order to buy souvenirs?
English.second-intro: Ask: in order to buy souvenirs?
English.question: in order to buy souvenirs?

English.first-intro: He corrects you and says: no, in order to buy gifts
English.second-intro: Answer: no, in order to buy gifts
English.question: no, in order to buy gifts

English.first-intro: Miguel tells you where he is going: I’m going to go to a sporting goods store
English.second-intro: Say: I’m going to go to a sporting goods store
English.question: I’m going to go to a sporting goods store

Spanish.answer: Voy a ir a una tienda de artículos deportivos

English.first-intro: He explains why the sports shop makes sense: my older son likes football
English.second-intro: Say: my older son likes football
English.question: my older son likes football

Spanish.grammar: a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object, possessive adjective
Spanish.answer: A mi hijo mayor le gusta el fútbol

English.first-intro: Miguel thinks the store may help him, how would he say: maybe there are gifts for my children in the store
English.second-intro: Try saying: maybe there are gifts for my children in the store
English.question: maybe there are gifts for my children in the store

Spanish.grammar: subjunctive with quizás, hay, possessive adjective
Spanish.answer: Quizás haya regalos para mis hijos en la tienda

English.first-intro: You ask Miguel about his wife: and for your wife, do you need something for her?
English.second-intro: Ask him: and for your wife, do you need something for her?
English.question: and for your wife, do you need something for her?

Spanish.grammar: question word, possessive adjective, para + person
Spanish.answer: Y para su mujer, ¿necesita algo para ella?

English.first-intro: Miguel is unsure what to choose. Here is how he says: I don’t know what
English.second-intro: Say: I don’t know what
English.question: I don’t know what

English.first-intro: Now have him answer more fully: yes, but I don’t know what
English.second-intro: Answer: yes, but I don’t know what
English.question: yes, but I don’t know what

English.first-intro: The gift search turns to Miguel's wife. Here is one idea: a scarf
English.second-intro: How do you say: a scarf
English.question: a scarf

English.first-intro: Suggest the gift to him: maybe a scarf
English.second-intro: Suggest: maybe a scarf
English.question: maybe a scarf

English.first-intro: Make that gift more specific: a red scarf
English.second-intro: Say: a red scarf
English.question: a red scarf

English.first-intro: Miguel explains her taste: my wife likes the color red
English.second-intro: Say: my wife likes the color red
English.question: my wife likes the color red

Spanish.grammar: a + person clarifier, gustar with indirect object, possessive adjective
Spanish.answer: A mi mujer le gusta el color rojo

English.first-intro: Offer another gift idea: or a silver bracelet
English.second-intro: Say: or a silver bracelet
English.question: or a silver bracelet

English.first-intro: Ask Miguel about combining the ideas: how about a pretty red scarf and a bracelet?
English.second-intro: Ask: how about a pretty red scarf and a bracelet?
English.question: how about a pretty red scarf and a bracelet?

Spanish.answer: ¿Qué tal una bufanda roja bonita y una pulsera?

English.first-intro: Miguel hesitates before deciding: maybe I am going to think about it
English.second-intro: Say: maybe I am going to think about it
English.question: maybe I am going to think about it

Spanish.grammar: ir a + infinitive, infinitive + object pronoun, subjunctive with quizás

English.first-intro: Back in the sports shop, tell the clerk what you need: I’m looking for a T-shirt of a football team
English.second-intro: Tell the clerk: I’m looking for a T-shirt of a football team
English.question: I’m looking for a T-shirt of a football team

Spanish.answer: Busco una camiseta de un equipo de fútbol

English.first-intro: The clerk asks for the size: what size?
English.second-intro: Ask: what size?
English.question: what size?

English.first-intro: Answer with the likely size: probably size m
English.second-intro: Say: probably size m
English.question: probably size m

English.first-intro: The clerk starts talking about choices. Here is the word for: an option
English.second-intro: How do you say: an option
English.question: an option

English.first-intro: Now learn the phrase: many options
English.second-intro: Say: many options
English.question: many options

English.first-intro: The clerk tells you about availability: in size m there are many choices
English.second-intro: Say: in size m there are many choices
English.question: in size m there are many choices

Spanish.answer: En la talla m hay muchas opciones

English.first-intro: She adds that the colors are available too: in those colors there are many options
English.second-intro: Add: in those colors there are many options
English.question: in those colors there are many options

Spanish.answer: En esos colores, hay muchas opciones

English.first-intro: Describe the team colors you mean: the Spanish team, yellow and red
English.second-intro: Say: the Spanish team, yellow and red
English.question: the Spanish team, yellow and red

Spanish.answer: El equipo español, amarillo y rojo

English.first-intro: Now say the store has many choices in those colors: there are many options in yellow and red
English.second-intro: Say: there are many options in yellow and red
English.question: there are many options in yellow and red

Spanish.answer: Hay muchas opciones en amarillo y rojo

English.first-intro: Later, you and Miguel plan the next day. Ask him: what do you want to do tomorrow?
English.second-intro: Ask him: what do you want to do tomorrow?
English.question: what do you want to do tomorrow?

English.first-intro: Miguel answers and turns the question back: I don’t know, and you?
English.second-intro: Answer: I don’t know, and you?
English.question: I don’t know, and you?

Spanish.grammar: direct object pronoun, negation, tag question ¿y tú?

English.first-intro: You mention there are several choices: various options
English.second-intro: Say: various options
English.question: various options

English.first-intro: Say that you both have choices: we have various options
English.second-intro: Say: we have various options
English.question: we have various options

English.first-intro: Suggest one idea to Miguel: how about a football match?
English.second-intro: Suggest: how about a football match?
English.question: how about a football match?

English.first-intro: Miguel rejects that plan because of the weather: no, it’s too hot
English.second-intro: Answer: no, it’s too hot
English.question: no, it’s too hot

English.first-intro: He asks what else you could do: what are the other options?
English.second-intro: Ask: what are the other options?
English.question: what are the other options?

English.first-intro: Shorten that question to: which?
English.second-intro: Ask: which?
English.question: which?

English.first-intro: Offer two alternatives: we could go to a park or to the beach
English.second-intro: Say: we could go to a park or to the beach
English.question: we could go to a park or to the beach

Spanish.answer: Podríamos ir a un parque o a la playa

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel the choice is his: you decide
English.second-intro: Say: you decide
English.question: you decide

English.first-intro: Miguel points to Elena, your travel companion. Here is how he says: tell her to decide
English.second-intro: Tell him: tell her to decide
English.question: tell her to decide

Spanish.grammar: imperative, subjunctive with que, a + person clarifier

English.first-intro: Elena chooses, and you agree: ok, let’s go to the beach
English.second-intro: Say: ok, let’s go to the beach
English.question: ok, let’s go to the beach

English.first-intro: Say what each of you can do there: we can swim and you can fish
English.second-intro: Say: we can swim and you can fish
English.question: we can swim and you can fish

Spanish.answer: Podemos nadar y tú puedes pescar

English.first-intro: The beach plan is set, and you are getting ready. Say: and I can put on my new bathing suit
English.second-intro: Say: and I can put on my new bathing suit
English.question: and I can put on my new bathing suit

Spanish.grammar: reflexive verb, poder + infinitive, possessive adjective
Spanish.answer: Y yo puedo ponerme mi bañador nuevo

English.first-intro: Describe it more fully: my new red bathing suit
English.second-intro: Say: my new red bathing suit
English.question: my new red bathing suit

English.first-intro: Put the full idea together: I can put on my new red bathing suit
English.second-intro: Say: I can put on my new red bathing suit
English.question: I can put on my new red bathing suit

Spanish.grammar: reflexive verb, poder + infinitive, possessive adjective
Spanish.answer: Puedo ponerme mi bañador rojo nuevo

English.first-intro: Say why the plan works: going to the beach is a good option
English.second-intro: Say: going to the beach is a good option
English.question: going to the beach is a good option

Spanish.answer: Ir a la playa es una buena opción

English.first-intro: Miguel mentions the area has choices: there are various beautiful beaches
English.second-intro: Say: there are various beautiful beaches
English.question: there are various beautiful beaches

English.first-intro: Add where they are: there are various beautiful beaches near here
English.second-intro: Say: there are various beautiful beaches near here
English.question: there are various beautiful beaches near here

Spanish.answer: Hay varias playas bonitas cerca de aquí

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel he can choose the specific beach: you decide what beach
English.second-intro: Say: you decide what beach
English.question: you decide what beach

English.first-intro: Miguel starts thinking about lunch after fishing. Here is the food word: fish (in reference to food)
English.second-intro: How do you say: fish (in reference to food)
English.question: fish (in reference to food)

English.first-intro: He adds a hopeful phrase: if I’m lucky
English.second-intro: Say: if I’m lucky
English.question: if I’m lucky

English.first-intro: Say what you might eat later: we’re going to eat fish
English.second-intro: Say: we’re going to eat fish
English.question: we’re going to eat fish

English.first-intro: Put the condition and plan together: if I’m lucky, we’re going to eat fish
English.second-intro: Put it together: if I’m lucky, we’re going to eat fish
English.question: if I’m lucky, we’re going to eat fish

Spanish.answer: Si tengo suerte, vamos a comer pescado

English.first-intro: Tell Miguel about your hobbies: I like to fish and to swim
English.second-intro: Say: I like to fish and to swim
English.question: I like to fish and to swim

Spanish.grammar: gustar with indirect object, present tense

English.first-intro: Say you have another swimming option: I can swim at the hotel
English.second-intro: Say: I can swim at the hotel
English.question: I can swim at the hotel

English.first-intro: You remember the hotel facilities. Here is how to say: they have a big swimming pool
English.second-intro: Say: they have a big swimming pool
English.question: they have a big swimming pool

English.first-intro: Contrast the hotel pool with your preference: but I prefer to go to the beach
English.second-intro: Say: but I prefer to go to the beach
English.question: but I prefer to go to the beach

Spanish.answer: Pero prefiero ir a la playa

English.first-intro: Miguel asks about where you are staying. First learn: you’re staying (there)
English.second-intro: How do you say: you’re staying (there)
English.question: you’re staying (there)

English.first-intro: Now have Miguel ask the full question: what hotel are you staying at?
English.second-intro: Ask: what hotel are you staying at?
English.question: what hotel are you staying at?

Spanish.grammar: question word, reflexive verb, present tense

English.first-intro: Answer him with your hotel: I’m at the Hilton Hotel
English.second-intro: Answer: I’m at the Hilton Hotel
English.question: I’m at the Hilton Hotel

English.first-intro: Miguel recognizes it and replies: oh yes, I know it
English.second-intro: Say: oh yes, I know it
English.question: oh yes, I know it

English.first-intro: He adds his opinion of it: it’s a very good hotel
English.second-intro: Say: it’s a very good hotel
English.question: it’s a very good hotel

English.first-intro: Miguel remembers one detail about the hotel: there’s a big aquarium in the lobby
English.second-intro: Say: there’s a big aquarium in the lobby
English.question: there’s a big aquarium in the lobby

Spanish.answer: Hay un acuario grande en el vestíbulo

English.first-intro: That evening, the conversation returns to the hotel. Here is how you say: tonight there’s a flamenco festival at the hotel
English.second-intro: Say: tonight there’s a flamenco festival at the hotel
English.question: tonight there’s a flamenco festival at the hotel

Spanish.answer: Esta noche hay un festival de flamenco en el hotel

English.first-intro: Miguel tells you about the performer: the flamenco dancer is very well known
English.second-intro: Say: the flamenco dancer is very well known
English.question: the flamenco dancer is very well known

English.first-intro: Finish by saying how you feel about it: I like the flamenco
English.second-intro: Say: I like the flamenco
English.question: I like the flamenco

Spanish.grammar: gustar with indirect object, present tense
